attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old footballer presents to the Emergency Department with a painful knee. He has been brought into the hospital by the team trainer, after being stretchered off the ground approximately an hour ago. Upon discussion of the mechanism of the injury, the footballer explained he planted his leg to pivot in the opposite direction and felt his knee 'give way and pop'. He felt immediate pain in the knee '8/10' which has now subsided to a '3/10'. He was initially unable to weight bear, but has now managed to walk from the waiting room to the treating bed with aid of the team trainer with clear favouring of the non-injured side.

On examination of the knee, there is clear evidence of swelling. There is no significant deformity, bruising or break of the skin. Palpation of the knee demonstrates tenderness of the medial and lateral joint lines, no tenderness of the patella, tibial tuberosity or head of the fibula. Active flexion of the knee can be completed between 0-70 degrees, however is inhibited by pain and swelling. Extension of the knee back to 0 degrees can be completed.

Valgus and Varus testing of the knee is negative. McMurry's test is difficult to complete due to pain and swelling. Lachman's test is positive, as is the anterior draw test. Posterior draw test is negative.

The patient is sent for an MRI (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is this type of injury most commonly associated with?
attributes.explanation:
The history is highly suggestive of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury. The patient's description of planting the injured leg and pivoting with weight through the joint with a popping and/or giving way sensation in the knee is a classic history explanation. On examination, Lachman's and the anterior draw test are positive in ACL injuries, as the function of the ACL is to stabilise the knee joint - inhibiting the lower leg from moving anterior in relation to the distal femur.
The MRI findings are as follows that are suggestive of an ACL injury:

  • Mid part of the ACL is discontinuous (ruptured), which is the most common aspect of the ACL to rupture (Image)

  • Malalignment of the ACL (usually more vertical than observed)

  • Anterior translocation of the tibia

ACL tears are comonly associated with:

  • O'Donoghue unhappy triad

  • Segond fractures

  • Posteromedial cornery injury of the knee

  • Meniscocapsular separation

A segond fracture is an avulsion fracture of the lateral aspect of the tibial plateau associated with ACL ligament tears in ~75% of cases. While ACL tears typically involve valgus stress, a segond fracture occurs as a result of internal rotation and varus stress as in this case.

Patella fracture are commonly caused by direct trauma to the patella or sudden forceful contraction of the quadriceps muscle. They are not associated with ACL tears but rather associated with ACL reconstruction and total knee reconstruction procedures.

Patellar tendinopathy is usually a pathology that occurs over a period of time and is not associated with an acute injury such as an ACL tear.

Common peroneal nerve neuropathy typically results from direct trauma to the lateral aspect of the knee or compression at the fibular head. It presents with foot drop and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot, neither of which are present in this case. It is not a recognised association with ACL tears.

Semimembranous sprain would present with tenderness over the posteromedial aspect of the knee and pain with resisted knee flexion. While hamstring injuries can occur with the same mechanism as ACL tears, isolated semimembranous injury is not a recognised association with ACL rupture. The positive Lachman's test and anterior drawer test specifically indicate ACL pathology rather than hamstring injury.

Mechanism of injury to the patella tendon is most commonly application of quadriceps with a flexed knee joint. Examination findings demonstrate a raised patella with ecchymosis and tenderness of palpation of the tibial tuberosity and inferior aspect of patella. The MRI, does not demonstrate any injury to the patella tendon.

The mechanism of injury being absent of significant force being applied to the thigh/leg through contact injury making distal femur injury much less likely. A significant amount of force is required to injure the distal femur via fracture. The patient is also much less likely to be able to weight bear on the injury.

There is some evidence of oedema of the tibial plateau on the MRI scan, however tibial plateau fractures are more commonly injured when external force is applied to the leg and is not classically seen in the reported history and examination findings.
learningPoints: Mechanism of ACL injury is classically described as a 'pop' or the knee 'giving way'
Lachman's test is positive in ACL rupture
ACL tears are associated with Segond fractures
references: Evans J, Nielson Jl. Anterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injuries. [Updated 2022 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499848/

Ng WH, Griffith JF, Hung EH, Paunipagar B, Law BK, Yung PS. Imaging of the anterior cruciate ligament. World J Orthop. 2011 Aug 18;2(8):75-84. doi: 10.5312/wjo.v2.i8.75. PMID: 22474639; PMCID: PMC3302044.
Chiu SS. The anterior tibial translocation sign. Radiology. 2006;239 (3): 914-5. doi:10.1148/radiol.2393040273
Silverstone L. Segond Fracture. Radiopaedia. Updated on Feburary 14 2023. Avaiable from: https://radiopaedia.org/articles/segond-fracture?lang=gb
